subject: Chemistry
title: Antivenom: Fighting Poison with Poison
description: A quick win: understand Antivenom: Fighting Poison with Poison.

hook.text: To make antivenom, scientists inject horses with tiny doses of venom. The horse makes antibodies. We harvest them.

buildup.text: Snake venom is a complex cocktail of proteins. Each species has a unique mix of toxins.

discovery.text: The horse's immune system produces antibodies that recognize and bind to specific venom proteins, neutralizing them.

twist.text: Antivenom from one snake species often doesn't work against another. You need the right antivenom for the right bite.

climax.text: Antivenom costs $14,000 per vial in the US. A severe bite can need 20+ vials—totaling hundreds of thousands of dollars.

punchline.text: Horse antibodies neutralize snake proteins. Expensive, specific, life-saving.

quiz.question: How is antivenom typically produced?
options: By injecting animals with small doses of venom and harvesting their antibodies
By synthesizing the venom's antidote in a lab
By extracting neutralizing compounds from immune snakes
